lessons.title: The Court & The Game
lessons.subtitle: Understanding the playing field
lessons.content: Volleyball is played on an 18m x 9m court divided by a net. Two teams of 6 players compete to send the ball over the net and land it in the opponent's court. Each team gets up to 3 touches before sending it back. A match is best of 5 sets — first to 25 points (with a 2-point lead) wins a set.
key_facts: Court size: 18m × 9m
6 players per team on court
Max 3 touches per possession
First to 25 points wins a set
The net height is 2.43m (men) / 2.24m (women)
lessons.selection_prompt: Which part of the court would you most want to play on?

lessons.title: The Serve
lessons.subtitle: Starting every rally
lessons.content: Every point begins with a serve. The server stands behind the end line and hits the ball into the opponent's court. There are two main types: the underhand serve (easier, less power) and the overhand serve (more power and control). Advanced players use a jump serve for maximum speed and angle.
key_facts: Server must stand behind the end line
Only 8 seconds to serve after whistle
Ball must clear the net without touching it
Underhand: hit ball with open fist from below
Overhand: toss ball and strike with open palm
lessons.selection_prompt: Which serve would you try first?

lessons.title: The Pass (Bump)
lessons.subtitle: The foundation of defense
lessons.content: The forearm pass (or 'bump') is the most fundamental skill in volleyball. You join your forearms together to create a flat platform and contact the ball with your forearms — NOT your hands. It's used to receive serves, dig attacks, and control the ball. Good passing is the #1 key to winning.
key_facts: Join forearms flat — no interlocked fingers
Bend your knees and get low
Contact ball on your forearms, not wrists
Aim to pass the ball up to the setter
Move your feet to get behind the ball
lessons.selection_prompt: What feels most challenging about passing?

lessons.title: The Set
lessons.subtitle: The art of the setup
lessons.content: The set is typically the second touch, delivered by the setter. Using fingertips (not palms), the setter pushes the ball upward and forward to position it perfectly for an attacker. It's the most precise skill in volleyball — a great set makes spiking much easier. The setter is like the quarterback of the team.
key_facts: Use fingertips, never the palm
Hands form a 'window' above your forehead
Push the ball — don't catch and throw it
Setter runs the team's offense
A set should hang in the air near the net
lessons.selection_prompt: Would you want to be a setter?
selection_options: Yes, I love being in control

lessons.title: The Spike (Attack)
lessons.subtitle: The most exciting play
lessons.content: The spike (or attack) is the third touch — a powerful hit aimed to score a point. The attacker takes a 3-step approach, jumps, and drives the ball downward into the opponent's court. It's the most spectacular play in volleyball. A spike can travel over 100 km/h at the professional level!
key_facts: 3-step approach: left-right-left (for right-handers)
Swing your arms back before jumping
Contact ball at the highest point
Snap your wrist to angle the ball down
Aim for open spots or weak defenders
lessons.selection_prompt: Where would you aim your spike?

quiz.question: How many players are on the court per team in volleyball?
quiz.explanation: Each team has 6 players on the court at a time.

quiz.question: How many touches is each team allowed per possession?
quiz.explanation: Teams get a maximum of 3 touches before sending the ball back over the net.

quiz.question: Which body part do you use for a forearm pass (bump)?
quiz.explanation: The forearm pass uses a flat platform formed by your forearms — not your hands or fists.

quiz.question: What is the role of the setter in volleyball?
options: To position the ball for an attacker
quiz.explanation: The setter delivers the second touch, setting up the ball near the net for an attacker to spike.

quiz.question: How many points does a team need to win a set (in most cases)?
quiz.explanation: A team needs 25 points (with a 2-point lead) to win a set. The 5th set, if played, goes to 15.

quiz.question: In an overhand serve, how do you contact the ball?
options: With a closed fist from below
With an open palm after tossing it up
quiz.explanation: An overhand serve involves tossing the ball in the air and striking it with an open palm.

quiz.question: What is the typical 3-step approach order for a right-handed spiker?
quiz.explanation: Right-handed players use a left-right-left (or sometimes right-left-right) approach. Left-right-left is most common.

quiz.question: Where must the server stand when serving?
options: In the middle of the back row
quiz.explanation: The server must always stand behind the end line (the back boundary) before and during the serve.

quiz.question: Which player usually wears a different color jersey?
quiz.explanation: The libero wears a different colored jersey and specializes in defense.
